narrative:
We remain in a moisture-rich atmosphere, and a front is pressing into the Southern Appalachians from the northwest. We have cloudy skies with showers developing from north to south this morning. An embedded thunderstorm is possible. Showers taper off by this evening. The front remains close enough Thursday to keep our weather unsettled — scattered showers with thunder possible. Fog is possible Thursday night into early Friday. Friday is drier, but showers are not completely out of play. Unseasonably cool weather remains with us Friday and Saturday.

In this week's Monday Message, we discuss drought amelioration and Gulf Tropical mischief. Thanks to our subscribers for your support!

2027 Blue Ridge Calendar

The 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar is available online and at retail outlets across the region. Thanks for a record-breaking pre-publication sale! You can see all the winning photography at our Photo Contest page. Check out volume discounts on the calendar and the "Mix and Match" sale on Drinkware.

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Hi: 76

High 76° Station sponsor Forecast Current Graphics Archives Webcams Showers Developing North to South Last Updated Wed July 22 at 10:57AM by Ray Russell We remain in a moisture-rich atmosphere, and a front is pressing into the Southern Appalachians from the northwest. We have cloudy skies with showers developing from north to south this morning. An embedded thunderstorm is possible. Showers taper off by this evening. The front remains close enough Thursday to keep our weather unsettled — scattered showers with thunder possible. Fog is possible Thursday night into early Friday. Friday is drier, but showers are not completely out of play. Unseasonably cool weather remains with us Friday and Saturday. In this week's Monday Message, we discuss drought amelioration and Gulf Tropical mischief. Thanks to our subscribers for your support! 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar The 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar is available online and at retail outlets across the region. Subscribe Tuesday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion A front presses in from the northwest today, working on very humid, tropical air. Showers develop from north to south through the morning with an embedded thunderstorm possible, tapering off during the evening. Clouds and rain hold temperatures below recent days. Tropical Storm Bertha never affects us directly, but she's in the news. She moves along the Louisiana coastline Wednesday and Wednesday night before drifting toward Texas. Her southerly feed of tropical moisture is part of why our air feels so soupy today. Thursday, the front stalls just to our south. With an easterly flow developing, steadier showers are mostly in south and east areas of the mountain region. For us, scattered showers with some thunder possible, and cooler. Friday is drier, but starts with some fog. A few showers and thundershowers pop up mostly in the afternoon. Saturday is the most difficult day of this forecast period. A disturbance moves through west to east with a few showers and thunder possible. Clouds decrease Sunday, but an isolated shower or thundershower is possible. Temperatures return to seasonal levels. High pressure gains a firmer hold on our weather Monday — dry and warm. But a front arrives late Tuesday with widely scattered showers and thunderstorms developing late-day. Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters. Subscribe Log in Ray Russell Founder & CEO PHOTO OF THE DAY CLICK TO ENLARGE Blazing Stars were abundant. Submitted by Rick Morris. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights Monday Message for July 20, 2026 In this week's Monday Message, we discuss the potential Tropical Storm Bertha and where she's headed, along with our continued gradual drought amelioration. Jul 20, 2026 Early July Heat, Revisited Update: Summer Is Hot We're not breaking any news here: it gets pretty hot in the summer around these parts. In the Foothills, 90s are quite common. The Hickory Airport has recorded 16 highs of 90F or greater from June 1st to July 15th, and the majority of the sub-90F days are in the mid and upper 80s. When you average out the temperatures over that same period, it has been a fairly run-of-the-mill summer. At long-term climate sites in vicinity of the Southern Appalachians, mean temperatures are all within 3 degrees of average, and within a degree or two at all of the sites within the Ray's Weather coverage area. Despite all of this relative ho-hum, the dog days of summer kicked off with a stretch of brutal heat and humidity that rivaled and exceeded several daily records across the region. By July 2nd, which was the first day that set any records, a stout upper-level ridge (often referred to in the media as a "heat dome") was positioned almost directly over the Southern Appalachians. The map above depicts the height of the 500-millibar pressure surface, in decameters, at 7 AM on July 2nd. The aforementioned "heat dome" is indicated by the closed brown circle over our region, with a 594 decameter contour. Any time we have abnormally high 500-millibar heights, we have above normal temperatures and, generally, dry weather. This next map depicting surface weather is quite busy, but all you really need to pay attention to is the Eastern half of the country. The looping brown contour from south Florida to east Texas and up through the Great Lakes vicinity with the two blue "H" symbols inside indicate a large area of surface high pressure dominating the eastern ~40% of the Lower 48. Surface high pressure generally promotes dry weather, and with the time of year and place of origin of this particular batch of high pressure, above normal temperatures can be expected as well. America 250, Deep Fried It seemed almost too perfect that America's 250th birthday would fall on a blazing hot Saturday. Take it from someone who was looking for a swimming hole that day: the lakes, creeks, and rivers of our area were about as crowded as they'll ever be, and for good reason. That cool mountain water and shady forest floor provided a refuge from the record heat of that weekend. Speaking of the records, let's dive into just how hot it got with a nice, colorful table. The McDonald's color palette was not an intentional choice, but perhaps a fitting one for America's 250th. The red boxes indicate a new daily record high temperature was recorded at that site on that date. The yellow boxes indicate that the record was tied. We looked at 12 reliable, permanent climate sites in our area. Unfortunately, not all of the data from one of our hottest sites (Morganton) was present. Morganton recieves the distinction of recording the hottest temperature throughout the area during this heat wave, with a blistering high of 103F on the 3rd, tying for the 4th highest temperature on record there. Records go back to 1893! The 3rd was the peak of the heat, with only Marshall failing to reach a record high temperature. Celo and Jefferson bested previous records by an incredible 6 degrees! Mount Mitchell came within 3 degrees of the all-time record high of 81F at 6844', but the heat was not done. July 4th, 2026 was another brutally hot day, with all but 2 of the 12 listed sites breaking or tying daily records. Just about everyone in the Foothills made it to triple digits, and Wytheville's heat peaked at a blazing 97 degrees. Another, more prominent record fell on the 4th, and that is the all-time high at Beech Mountain. Now, this does come with an asterisk: Beech Mountain's weather records only go to back to December of 1991. So, it is certainly possible there has been a temperature of 85F or higher at some point before then. But, what we have is what we have, and while it is a short period of record, 85F is the warmest temperature ever recorded on Beech. Boone's 90s Boone's weather reporting location has an interesting history. From New Year's Day of 1929 to May 31st, 1980, the reporting location was closer to downtown. Starting in 1980, the station was moved to the water treatment facility behind Watauga High, along the South Fork of the New River. This is not a major move in terms of distance, but there are some differences in weather in between those sites. Down along the river, the mornings will tend to be cooler in the summertime thanks to nighttime radiational cooling. Additionally, 40+ years of urban development means that Boone proper will be warmer than the water treatment facility, thanks to the urban heat island effect. All of this is to say that downtown Boone was likely a couple of degrees warmer than the official records will state. The two 90F readings on the 3rd and 4th are the 35th and 36th instances of a 90F high temperature in all the years of Boone weather reporting, regardless of station location. Our own station on the LifeStore building off of Blowing Rock Road recorded a high of 93.0F on the 2nd, and 91.2F on the 3rd. So, the difference is minimal, but one worth considering when splitting hairs like us scientists love to do. To most of y'all, it was just plain hot. Jul 16, 2026 June 2026 Recap Plain Old Summer We kicked off the summer of 2026 with a fairly typical month of June. Temperatures were fairly steady, and largely near or just above normal. Although scattered showers and thunderstorms were a regularity, we did not have many days where storms were especially widespread, and thus the vast majority of the region wound up below normal once again on monthly precipitation. We'll get more into that later on. The month started out rather pleasantly, with most stations notching their coolest low temperature on the morning of the 3rd. Only a handful of stations failed to drop below 50 degrees that morning, which is a fairly impressive chill for the time of year. In southern Ashe County, Todd managed to drop to 38.5F before rallying to nearly 74 degrees for the high temperature, which is the sort of day we expect to see in late spring. Nearby in Boone, the morning low of 39F got within 3 degrees of the record low for the date (36F, set in 1956). That 39F is around 13 degrees below normal for early June. Down in Black Mountain, 23 of 30 days in June reached at least 80 degrees, and 4 made it to 90F. Two mornings managed to stay at 70F or more, which is a fairly impressive level of warmth for a sunrise in the valley at 2,300 feet. Like many other stations, a decent chunk of the month's rainfall came in a few days towards the end of the month, as 0.92" fell in 3 days - close to half of the monthly total there. Surry Co. Slammers As you will see later on in the precipitation section of this recap, only one of our major stations exceeded their monthly average rainfall: the Surry County Airport near Mount Airy. In fact, two individual days, the 10th and the 18th, produced 4.52 inches of rain, which would have been the most of any of the stations included in the precipitation table even without adding in the other 28 days of the month! Summer is a time of "winners and losers" when it comes to rainfall, which is illustrated even further by the maps below (courtesy of NOAA). The Surry Co. Airport picked up 2.45" on the 10th in the image on the left, while just a couple miles up the road closer to the town of Mt. Airy, less than half of that fell. Not to mention that southern and western Surry County only recorded 0.01"! A more widespread rainfall occurred on the 18th, with other stations nearby exceeding 2 inches along with the airport site. Once you add in the other 28 days of June, the Surry Co. Airport recorded 6.23 inches of rain, just over 125 percent of a normal June. The next highest percentage in our recap? 81.0 percent on Beech Mountain. This time around, Mount Airy was the winner for hit-or-miss thunderstorms. Temperatures June did not bring much that fell very far outside the envelope of "ordinary" this year. The aforementioned chill of the morning of June 3rd brought some of the greatest departures from normal for the month, but outside of that, things stayed within 10 or so degrees of normal. Now you may think upon reading that statement: "Well, of course they stayed close to normal. That's why it is called normal!" A fair reaction, to be certain. The deep summer months of June, July, August, and even September to some degree, do not bring quite as many moments with temperatures departing the averages as fall, winter, and spring do. If you go and read one of our recaps from the any of those months, you will see that the averages are relatively far from the experienced temperatures with some regularity. In summer, cold fronts are weak and transient, bringing only minor "cooldowns." It is easier to get far away from average with excessive heat, as we did to start July. To illustrate this, take a look at the data from the Hickory Regional Airport in June. Only one out of thirty high temperatures and two out of thirty low temperatures in June were 10 or more degrees above or below normal, combining for 3/60 highs & lows. The 57 other highs/lows were within 10 degrees of average, and many were within 4 to 6 degrees. Back in April, 25 out of 60 highs or lows were 10 or more degrees away from average! The table and map below help illustrate the point further. Things just did not get very far from what you expect June to be, and they rarely ever do. The largest departures from normal lie with the low temperatures in the High Country being a decent bit above normal.

Precipitation As was discussed above, summer is the time of winners and losers when it comes to rainfall. Most were losers this time around. Burnsville, Jefferson, Lenoir, Mroganton, and North Wilkesboro all fell shy of 2 inches of rain for the month, while Beech Mountain recieved 4 inches (on the dot!) and Mt. Airy's good luck pushed them past 6 inches. The "biggest loser" for the month was Morganton, where only 27.1% of average June rainfall occurred. We had many days where storms favored the mountains (as they often do, thanks to the terrain creating better opoortunities to spark storms), and Morganton failed to cash in on the scattered chances in the Foothills. The map below displays the luck of Mount Airy compared to the rest of Surry County... northwestern portions of the county recieved less than 50% of average precipitation for the month! Our only other contender for above average precipitation was Wolf Laurel, where over 5 inches of rain fell. Again, all thanks to their good luck on seeing afternoon storms up on the ridgeline.

; Precipitation; As was discussed above, summer is the time of winners and losers when it comes to rainfall. Most were losers this time around. Burnsville, Jefferson, Lenoir, Mroganton, and North Wilkesboro all fell shy of 2 inches of rain for the month, while Beech Mountain recieved 4 inches (on the dot!) and Mt. Airy's good luck pushed them past 6 inches. The "biggest loser" for the month was Morganton, where only 27.1% of average June rainfall occurred. We had many days where storms favored the mountains (as they often do, thanks to the terrain creating better opoortunities to spark storms), and Morganton failed to cash in on the scattered chances in the Foothills.; The map below displays the luck of Mount Airy compared to the rest of Surry County... northwestern portions of the county recieved less than 50% of average precipitation for the month! Our only other contender for above average precipitation was Wolf Laurel, where over 5 inches of rain fell. Again, all thanks to their good luck on seeing afternoon storms up on the ridgeline.;
